title: Severe Bleeding
firstAid: Apply direct, firm pressure using the cleanest cloth available or sterile gauze.
If blood soaks through, do NOT remove the first cloth; add another layer on top and keep pressing.
Maintain pressure for at least 10-15 minutes without lifting to check.
Keep the injured limb elevated above heart level to reduce blood flow.
If the bleeding is from a limb and does not stop, consider a tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound (if trained).
Call emergency services (108) immediately and provide exact GPS coordinates.
Monitor for signs of shock (pale skin, rapid pulse, confusion).
avoid: Do not remove embedded objects (glass, metal) as they may be acting as a plug.
Do not use dirty or fuzzy materials like cotton wool which stick to the wound.
Do not remove dressings once applied.

title: Unconscious Person
firstAid: Check the scene for safety, then check for a response (Shout and Tap).
Check for breathing (look, listen, feel) for no more than 10 seconds.
If breathing: Place them in the RECOVERY POSITION (on their side with head tilted back).
If NOT breathing: Start CPR immediately (30 chest compressions, 2 rescue breaths).
Clear the mouth of any obstructions or vomit.
Loosen tight clothing around the neck and waist.
Call for an ambulance and stay with the victim until help arrives.
avoid: Do not leave the person alone.
Do not give any food, water, or medicine by mouth.
Do not place a pillow under their head as it may block the airway.

title: Cardiac Arrest
firstAid: Confirm unconsciousness and lack of pulse/breathing.
Call emergency services immediately and request an AED (Automated External Defibrillator).
Start CPR: Push hard and fast in the center of the chest (100-120 compressions per minute).
Allow the chest to recoil completely between compressions.
If an AED arrives, follow the voice prompts immediately—it can reset the heart rhythm.
Switch with another person every 2 minutes to maintain high-quality compressions.
avoid: Do not stop CPR until paramedics arrive or the victim shows signs of life.
Do not hesitate to use an AED—it will not shock unless necessary.

title: Breathing Difficulty
firstAid: Help the person sit upright in a comfortable position (tripod position).
Loosen clothing around the neck, chest, and waist.
If the person has prescribed medication (like an inhaler), help them use it.
Keep the person calm to reduce oxygen demand.
Monitor for bluish tint on lips or fingernails (cyanosis).
Call 108 if symptoms persist or worsen.
Be prepared to start CPR if they stop breathing.
avoid: Do not force the victim to lie flat as it makes breathing harder.
Do not give them water or food if they are struggling for air.

title: Head Injury
firstAid: Keep the person completely still and minimize neck movement.
Apply light pressure to any scalp bleeding with a clean cloth, unless a skull fracture is suspected.
If fluid is leaking from ears or nose, do NOT try to stop it—cover it lightly.
Monitor for 'Red Flags': Pupil inequality, seizures, repeated vomiting, or loss of memory.
Keep the victim awake and talk to them to assess consciousness levels.
Seek immediate neuro-emergency care.
avoid: Do not move the head or neck.
Do not apply direct pressure if the skull feels 'mushy' or indented (possible fracture).
Do not give pain medication like Aspirin as it can increase brain bleeding.

title: Spinal Injury
firstAid: Assume a spinal injury if there was a high-speed crash or fall.
Tell the victim: 'DO NOT MOVE. Keep your head and body perfectly still.'
Place heavy items (like bags or folded jackets) on both sides of the neck to prevent turning.
Do NOT attempt to move the victim unless they are in immediate danger (e.g., car fire).
If they must be moved, use the 'Log Roll' technique with at least 3 people.
Monitor breathing and pulse continuously.
avoid: Do not bend the neck, back, or spine.
Do not remove a helmet (e.g., motorcycle) unless it is blocking the airway.
Do not try to sit the person up.

title: Bone Fracture
firstAid: Control any bleeding first using sterile dressings.
Immobilize the area: Use a splint (cardboard, wood, or the body itself) to stop movement.
Apply the splint ABOVE and BELOW the joint nearest the fracture.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for 20 minutes to reduce swelling.
Check for 'Pulse, Sensation, and Color' below the injury to ensure circulation.
Elevate the limb if possible to reduce pain and swelling.
avoid: Do not try to 'push' the bone back in or straighten it.
Do not test the injury by trying to walk on it.
Do not apply ice directly to the skin.

title: Burn Injury
firstAid: Stop the burning process: Remove from heat or douse flames.
Cool the burn: Run cool (not cold) tap water over the area for at least 20 minutes.
Remove jewelry or tight clothing from the area before it starts to swell.
Cover the burn loosely with sterile non-stick gauze or clean plastic wrap.
For severe burns, treat for shock (lay flat, elevate legs slightly).
Call emergency services for burns on the face, hands, or large areas.
avoid: Do not use ice, butter, ointment, or toothpaste on a burn.
Do not break blisters as it increases infection risk.
Do not remove clothing that is stuck to the burn.

title: Internal Bleeding
firstAid: Recognize symptoms: Painful/rigid abdomen, coughing blood, or bruising in the torso.
Call 108 immediately—this is a surgical emergency.
Lay the person flat and keep them warm with a blanket.
Raise their legs 6-12 inches to maintain blood flow to the brain.
Monitor for loss of consciousness.
Stay with the person and provide reassurance.
avoid: Do not give anything to eat or drink (they may need surgery).
Do not ignore minor-looking bruises if the impact was severe.

title: Shock
firstAid: Lay the person down and elevate their legs about 12 inches.
Keep the person warm with a blanket or jacket.
Loosen any restrictive clothing around the neck and chest.
Treat any underlying cause (like bleeding or burns) immediately.
Reassure the person and keep them as calm as possible.
Monitor breathing and pulse every few minutes.
avoid: Do not let the person eat or drink.
Do not move them if you suspect a spinal injury.
Do not give them an alcoholic drink.

title: Concussion
firstAid: Ensure the person stops the activity immediately.
Apply a cold pack to the site of impact.
Monitor for 24-48 hours for worsening symptoms.
Seek IMMEDIATE care if: Seizures, slurred speech, or persistent vomiting occur.
Ensure the person rests both physically and mentally (no screens).
avoid: Do not allow the person to return to activity/driving.
Do not give Aspirin as it can worsen potential brain bleeding.
Do not allow them to sleep for more than 2 hours without checking their responsiveness.

title: Chest Injury
firstAid: Sit the person up and lean them slightly toward the injured side.
If there is an open wound (sucking chest wound), cover it with a plastic dressing taped on 3 sides.
Monitor for tension pneumothorax (extreme difficulty breathing).
Support the chest with a pillow if it helps with pain.
Call 108 immediately.
avoid: Do not tape all 4 sides of an open chest wound (can trap air).
Do not ignore 'crackling' sounds in the skin.

title: Eye Injury
firstAid: Chemicals: Flush the eye with clean water for at least 15-20 minutes continuously.
Foreign Object: Do NOT rub. Try to blink it out or flush with water.
Puncture: Cover the eye with a rigid shield (like a paper cup) without touching the eye.
Cover the UNINJURED eye too to stop both eyes from moving.
Seek emergency ophthalmology care.
avoid: Do not rub the eye.
Do not try to remove an object embedded in the eyeball.
Do not apply pressure to the eyeball.

title: Panic Attack
firstAid: Move the person to a quiet, safe area away from the crash site.
Encourage 'Box Breathing': Inhale for 4s, Hold for 4s, Exhale for 4s, Hold for 4s.
Speak in a calm, low, and reassuring voice.
Acknowledge their feelings without judgment.
Stay with them until their breathing returns to normal.
avoid: Do not tell them to 'calm down' or 'relax'—be specific with breathing cues.
Do not panic yourself.

title: Airway Blockage (Choking)
firstAid: If the person can cough or speak, encourage them to keep coughing.
If they CANNOT breathe/speak: Perform the Heimlich Maneuver (Abdominal Thrusts).
Give 5 back blows followed by 5 abdominal thrusts.
Repeat until the object is expelled or the person becomes unconscious.
If unconscious: Start CPR and look for the object in the mouth.
avoid: Do not perform a 'blind finger sweep' as it may push the object deeper.
Do not give water until the airway is clear.

title: Collapsed Lung
firstAid: Keep the victim in a seated position (makes breathing easier).
Monitor for signs of oxygen deprivation (cyanosis).
Call 108 immediately—this requires a chest tube.
Keep the person calm to minimize respiratory distress.
avoid: Do not force the person to lie down.
Do not allow them to exert themselves.

title: Crush Injury
firstAid: Call emergency services immediately and specify it is a crush injury.
If the object has been on for >15 mins, do NOT remove it without medical supervision (Crush Syndrome).
If you MUST remove it, apply a tourniquet above the injury site first (if trained).
Control any external bleeding.
Provide warmth and reassurance.
avoid: Do not release the pressure suddenly if it has been applied for a long time.
Do not move the trapped limb.

title: Amputation
firstAid: CONTROL BLEEDING: Apply direct pressure and use a tourniquet if needed.
CARE FOR THE PART: Rinse with saline/clean water (do not scrub).
Wrap in sterile gauze, place in a sealed plastic bag.
Place the bag inside another container with ice and water.
Seek emergency surgery immediately.
avoid: Do not place the severed part directly on ice (causes tissue damage).
Do not use dry ice.

title: Whiplash Injury
firstAid: Support the neck with a soft collar or folded towel.
Apply ice for the first 24 hours to reduce inflammation.
Rest the neck and avoid sudden movements.
Seek medical evaluation to rule out fractures.
avoid: Do not force movement to 'stretch' the neck.
Do not ignore numbness in fingers.

title: Seizures
firstAid: Protect from injury: Move objects away from the victim.
Place something soft (like a jacket) under their head.
Time the seizure. If it lasts >5 mins, call 108.
Once shaking stops, turn them to the recovery position.
Loosen clothing around the neck.
avoid: Do not restrain the person.
Do not put ANYTHING in their mouth.
Do not give water until they are fully awake.

title: Hypothermia
firstAid: Move the person out of the cold/wet environment.
Remove wet clothing and replace with warm, dry layers.
Cover their head and neck.
Give warm, non-alcoholic, non-caffeinated drinks if they can swallow.
Monitor for slowed breathing.
avoid: Do not use direct heat like hot water bottles on skin.
Do not rub their limbs (can cause cardiac arrest).

title: Heat Stroke
firstAid: Move the person to a cool, shaded area.
Cool the body rapidly: Use cold water, ice packs on armpits/groin, or fans.
If conscious, give small sips of water.
Monitor temperature and consciousness.
Call 108 immediately—this is life-threatening.
avoid: Do not give Aspirin or Paracetamol.
Do not give caffeinated or alcoholic drinks.

title: Facial Fracture
firstAid: Apply a cold pack to reduce swelling.
If bleeding from the mouth, sit them forward to prevent choking.
Protect any loose teeth.
Seek emergency Maxillofacial care.
avoid: Do not blow the nose.
Do not try to 'snap' the nose back in place.

title: Deep Laceration
firstAid: Clean with running water (if not bleeding severely).
Apply firm pressure with a sterile pad.
Bandage tightly enough to hold pressure but not stop circulation.
Seek medical help for stitches within 6 hours.
avoid: Do not use hydrogen peroxide or alcohol on deep wounds.
Do not leave the wound open to the air.

title: Pelvic Fracture
firstAid: Treat as a spinal injury: DO NOT MOVE.
Support the hips with folded blankets on both sides.
Treat for shock (keep warm).
Monitor for internal bleeding signs.
Call 108 immediately.
avoid: Do not try to walk.
Do not move their legs.

title: Rib Fracture
firstAid: Help the person find a comfortable sitting position.
Encourage the person to take regular, shallow breaths to avoid lung collapse.
Apply ice to the area for 20 minutes.
Provide a pillow for them to hug against their chest during coughing.
Seek medical evaluation to check for lung puncture.
avoid: Do not wrap the chest tightly with bandages.
Do not encourage heavy physical activity.

title: Nose Bleeding
firstAid: Sit the person down and tilt their head SLIGHTLY FORWARD.
Pinch the soft part of the nose for 10-15 minutes without letting go.
Apply a cold pack to the bridge of the nose.
Instruct them to breathe through their mouth.
Seek help if it doesn't stop after 30 minutes.
avoid: Do not tilt the head backward (causes blood to enter the throat).
Do not blow the nose for several hours.

title: Vomiting After Injury
firstAid: Turn the person on their side immediately (Recovery Position) to prevent choking.
Clear the mouth of any vomit.
Monitor for signs of worsening concussion or internal head pressure.
Call for medical evaluation if vomiting is repeated.
avoid: Do not give anti-nausea medication without a doctor's order.
Do not let them lie flat on their back.

title: Dehydration
firstAid: Provide water or oral rehydration salts (ORS) in small, frequent sips.
Move to a shaded or air-conditioned area.
Remove excess clothing.
Apply a cool, damp cloth to the skin.
avoid: Do not give large amounts of water at once (can cause vomiting).
Do not give drinks with high caffeine or sugar.

title: Psychological Trauma
firstAid: Validate their feelings: 'It's okay to feel this way, you are safe now.'
Use 'Grounding Techniques': Ask them to name 5 things they see, 4 they hear, etc.
Keep them away from the visual scene of the accident.
Encourage professional counseling after the immediate emergency.
avoid: Do not minimize their experience by saying 'it could have been worse'.
Do not leave them alone.

title: Arterial Bleeding
firstAid: This is a high-speed leak: Apply HEAVY direct pressure immediately.
If a limb is involved, apply a tourniquet immediately 2-3 inches above the wound.
Do NOT wait for help to arrive before applying pressure.
Treat for shock (lay flat, elevate legs).
Call 108 immediately.
avoid: Do not try to 'wash' the wound first.
Do not use weak pressure.

title: Bruising
firstAid: Use the R.I.C.E protocol: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation.
Apply ice for 15-20 minutes every 2 hours.
If the bruise is on a limb, wrap it with an elastic bandage.
Seek help if the bruise is extremely large or painful.
avoid: Do not apply heat for the first 48 hours.
Do not try to 'drain' the bruise.

title: Dislocated Shoulder
firstAid: Immobilize the shoulder in its current position using a sling or towel.
Apply ice to the joint to reduce pain.
Monitor for numbness or tingling in the arm/fingers.
Seek emergency orthopedic care.
avoid: Do not try to 'pop' the shoulder back into the socket.
Do not allow the arm to hang unsupported.

title: Dislocated Knee
firstAid: This is a medical emergency as it can damage arteries.
Check for a pulse at the ankle.
Immobilize the entire leg in its current position.
Call 108 immediately.
avoid: Do not try to straighten the leg.
Do not apply heavy pressure to the knee.

title: Hand Injury
firstAid: Remove rings or watches before swelling starts.
Clean minor cuts with water.
Immobilize the wrist or fingers if a fracture is suspected.
Elevate the hand above the heart level.
avoid: Do not delay removing rings—they can cut off circulation.

title: Foot Injury
firstAid: Remove shoes and socks to inspect the injury.
Elevate the foot and apply ice.
If a toe is broken, buddy-tape it to the neighboring toe.
Seek evaluation for potential small bone fractures.
avoid: Do not continue to walk or stand on the injured foot.

title: Tendon Injury
firstAid: Immobilize the joint in a neutral position.
Apply ice and compression.
Avoid all weight-bearing on the affected limb.
Seek orthopedic evaluation.
avoid: Do not try to 'walk it off'.

title: Ligament Tear
firstAid: Follow the R.I.C.E protocol.
Use a brace or splint to provide stability.
Check for excessive swelling which may indicate a complete tear.
Rest is the primary treatment.
avoid: Do not return to activity until pain-free and stable.

title: Jaw Injury
firstAid: Support the jaw gently with a bandage wrapped under the chin and over the head.
Apply a cold pack to the side of the face.
Ask the person to avoid talking or opening their mouth wide.
Seek emergency care.
avoid: Do not try to force the jaw shut.

title: Tooth Damage
firstAid: Handle the tooth by the CROWN (top), never the root.
If dirty, rinse with milk or water (do not scrub).
Try to put the tooth back in the socket and have them bite down.
If not possible, keep it in a small container of milk.
Visit a dentist within 30-60 minutes.
avoid: Do not let the tooth dry out.
Do not touch the root.

title: Smoke Inhalation
firstAid: Get the victim to fresh air immediately.
Loosen clothing around the neck and chest.
If the victim is unconscious, start CPR if needed.
Oxygen therapy is usually required—call 108.
Watch for swelling of the throat/airway.
avoid: Do not stay in the smoke area to help unless you have breathing apparatus.

title: Vehicle Fire Burns
firstAid: Stop, Drop, and Roll if clothing is on fire.
Douse the person with large amounts of water immediately.
Remove burned clothing unless it is stuck to the skin.
Call 108 and treat for shock.
avoid: Do not use fire extinguishers directly on people if possible.
Do not apply ice.

title: Chemical Burns
firstAid: Brush off dry chemicals first (using gloves).
Flush the skin with running water for at least 20 minutes.
Remove all contaminated clothing and jewelry.
Cover with a sterile dressing.
Identify the chemical if possible for medical staff.
avoid: Do not use a high-pressure hose.
Do not try to neutralize the chemical with another substance (creates heat).

title: Fainting
firstAid: Lay the person flat on their back.
Elevate the feet about 12 inches.
Loosen restrictive clothing.
Check for any injuries sustained during the fall.
Provide fresh air.
avoid: Do not let them stand up too quickly.
Do not pour water on their face.

title: Anxiety Attack
firstAid: Help the victim focus on their breathing.
Acknowledge that it feels like a physical emergency but reassure them they are safe.
Provide a quiet space away from the crash scene.
Encourage them to breathe into their belly, not chest.
avoid: Do not tell them they are 'overreacting'.

title: Near Drowning
firstAid: Remove the victim from the water safely.
Check for breathing and pulse.
If not breathing: Start CPR immediately.
Keep them warm to prevent hypothermia.
Even if they recover, they MUST go to the hospital (Secondary Drowning risk).
avoid: Do not try to 'drain' water from the lungs—focus on CPR.

title: Allergic Reaction (Anaphylaxis)
firstAid: Help the person use their EpiPen if they have one.
Call 108 immediately.
Lay them flat with legs raised.
Keep the airway clear.
Monitor for a second wave of reaction.
avoid: Do not wait to see if symptoms get better.
Do not give oral allergy medicine if they can't swallow.

title: Airbag Impact
firstAid: Rinse any 'chemical burns' from the airbag powder with water.
Monitor for internal chest injuries or rib fractures.
Check vision if the airbag hit the face.
Seek medical evaluation for internal trauma.
avoid: Do not ignore chest pain even if no bruising is visible.

title: Glass Fragment
firstAid: If the glass is small/shallow, wash and remove with tweezers.
If LARGE or DEEP: DO NOT REMOVE.
Pad around the glass with bulky dressings to stop it from moving.
Seek surgical removal.
avoid: Do not press directly on the glass.

title: Road Rash
firstAid: Wash with clean water and mild soap if possible.
Gently remove loose gravel/dirt.
Apply a thin layer of antibiotic ointment.
Cover with a non-stick bandage.
Check for signs of infection (pus, redness) daily.
avoid: Do not scrub the wound aggressively.
Do not use harsh chemicals like iodine.
